rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source text: the roadmap/service path is prepared "to enable energyefficient construction." This directly supports reducing building energy use (mitigation) via improved building control across project stages. Despite the gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token found'), the climate_screen is 'explicit_self', so we keep the row. Primary category 'Mitigation' and mechanism 'building_controls_optimization → energy_use_reduction' are consistent with the statement.
policy_statement: Prepare a roadmap/service path for developing building control across all project stages to enable energy-efficient construction (pilots 2017–2021; experiences included by 2035).
mechanism_description: The city will prepare a roadmap/service path to develop building control across all construction project stages so new construction operates more energy-efficiently and uses less energy.
primary_causal_pathway: Building operations/control strategy → ↓ energy use (same service) → ↓ fuel combustion/electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Building design/commissioning/operations are adjusted via improved controls (e.g., HVAC control sequences, setpoints, scheduling, demand-controlled ventilation, optimized automation) across project stages so buildings run efficiently.  
(2) Mediating process: Better control logic and commissioning reduce wasted heating/cooling/ventilation/lighting energy while maintaining service levels, lowering delivered energy.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower final energy demand reduces upstream energy production and combustion, decreasing CO₂ (and potentially other) emissions associated with building energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy demand, leading to lower GHG emissions from electricity and heating supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Building control optimization reduces energy use for the same delivered building services, which reduces GHG emissions from energy supply—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although improved controls can enhance comfort and potentially reduce overheating risk (an adaptation co-benefit), the core intended and largest climate effect is energy and emissions reduction. It is not primarily “Resource Efficiency” in the materials/water sense, nor “Nature-Based Solutions.”  
3) Edge cases applied: If a controls strategy increased electricity use (e.g., shifting from gas to electric with higher kWh but lower emissions) the dominant climate outcome could still be mitigation via emissions reduction; however, the stated mechanism explicitly targets “uses less energy,” supporting energy-demand-driven mitigation.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy centered on optimizing building controls to reduce energy use shares the same primary mitigation pathway through reduced energy demand and associated emissions.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary tagging (e.g., demand management, fuel switching compatibility, resilience/comfort co-benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific implementation details and evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Controls optimization can sometimes shift load timing (demand response) or increase electricity use while reducing emissions via electrification; classification here is anchored to the mechanism’s stated goal of lowering energy use overall. Potential adaptation co-benefits (comfort/overheating management) are secondary unless explicitly dominant in a given policy.
key_indicators: “road map or service path will be prepared”; “development of building control”; “in all stages of a construction project”; “to enable energy-efficient construction”; “Pilots during… (2017–2021)”; “Experiences to be included by 2035”
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort and air quality; reduced energy costs; better building performance/quality assurance
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information/guidance tool, but the emphasis on a structured “roadmap/service path” across project stages aligns best with a planning-strategy rather than pure information-disclosure; no binding requirements are stated.
